evidence_quotes.quote: Visa/Mastercard/Paypal theatening platforms like steam or itch.io to erase game from their catalog despite being legal because they are considered content that have an image risk.
Sold digital products (games, movies, music or software) can be rendered inaccesssible for the customer who purchased it and may even have purchased microtransactions through it.
This inaccessibility is not planned and the customer has no choice in this that to losse what was bought.
The companies that sell these digital products should at least have an end of life process from the start to allow the paid customers to continue to have access to their purchase.

paragraphs.text: Hello, thank you for your work on this matter, it is very important and I strongly support it. Due to current growing concerns there are 2 points I would like to see added in the fairness act: - To avoid paiment processsors to impose anything regarding content (aside from being illegal of course) to any platform that uses them since these platforms have no other choice to survive. Visa/Mastercard/Paypal theatening platforms like steam or itch.io to erase game from their catalog despite being legal because they are considered content that have an image risk. Where will they stop? That is as if THEY decides what is legal and what is not. It is not a power they should have. - It would be good to add a degree of customer protection regarding the destruction of already sold videogames. games/digital products in general. Sold digital products (games, movies, music or software) can be rendered inaccesssible for the customer who purchased it and may even have purchased microtransactions through it. This inaccessibility is not planned and the customer has no choice in this that to losse what was bought. The companies that sell these digital products should at least have an end of life process from the start to allow the paid customers to continue to have access to their purchase. Thank you for taking this comment in consideration, hoping it is a reasonnable request.
